entities.name: dhvani
entities.cite: the apprehension of the sound is accepted

axioms.name: vyañjaka
axioms.cite: the sound is manifesting relative to the sphota

axioms.name: grahaṇa
axioms.cite: the apprehension of the sound is accepted

axioms.name: saṃband
axioms.cite: three distinct relationships hold between sound and apprehension

denials.name: anirūpita
denials.cite: the sound's role cannot remain unspecified in the theory of manifestation

claims.name: nitya
claims.cite: the question of apprehension remains disputed

rejected_readings.label: sound_as_primary
rejected_readings.rendering: Sound is a primary and independently perceivable physical phenomenon in hearing.
rejected_readings.why: This ignores the entire manifestation-theoretic frame that all three positions in the verse presuppose: sound is always grasped as manifesting the sphota, not as a self-standing object. Treating sound as phenomenologically primary reverses the hierarchy Bhartrhari maintains throughout.

rejected_readings.label: confusion_of_mechanism
rejected_readings.rendering: The three theories differ in what is perceived, not in how sound functions as manifesto
rejected_readings.why: The verse presents three theories that all agree sound manifests sphota but disagree about the phenomenological character of that manifestation: whether it is inseparable, completely hidden, or independently audible. Confusing the object of dispute (the mechanism) with the object itself (what is manifested) misreads the phenomenology.

notes: Verse 1.81 presents three competing phenomenological theories of sound-apprehension (avibhāgena, asaṃvedya, svatantra) while Bhartrhari remains uncommitted. The common ground is the manifestation-framework: all three presuppose dhvani's role as the manifesting medium. The dispute concerns only how that manifestation structures the hearer's experience. The contract captures this dual consensus-and-disagreement, reflecting the verse's doxographic method.
